Cells talking with electricity.<[silence:500ms]> No words, just voltage.<[silence:500ms]>

The term bioelectric communication describes how cells send signals using tiny electrical currents—like a network of silent walkie-talkies built into every living thing.<[silence:500ms]> Your nerves fire this way, but so do plants, fungi, and even single-celled slime molds.<[silence:500ms]>

Here’s how it works:<[silence:70ms]> Cells pump charged particles (ions) in and out, creating voltage differences across their membranes.<[silence:500ms]> When one cell’s voltage spikes, neighbors detect the change and respond—like dominoes falling without touch.<[silence:500ms]> A wounded leaf sends distress signals this way, warning other leaves to flood themselves with bitter chemicals before insects arrive.<[silence:500ms]> Cut a flatworm in half, and bioelectric patterns guide its head and tail to regrow in the right places.<[silence:500ms]> No brain needed.<[silence:500ms]>

Scientists now hack these signals.<[silence:500ms]> Apply the right voltage to a frog embryo, and it grows an extra eye on its gut.<[silence:500ms]> Block the wrong channels in a tumor, and cancer cells forget how to spread.<[silence:500ms]> It’s not genetics or chemistry doing the talking—it’s physics.<[silence:500ms]>

Three related subjects are regenerative medicine secrets, plant internet networks, and how slime solves mazes.<[silence:500ms]>
clean_story:
Cells talking with electricity. No words, just voltage.

The term bioelectric communication describes how cells send signals using tiny electrical currents—like a network of silent walkie-talkies built into every living thing. Your nerves fire this way, but so do plants, fungi, and even single-celled slime molds.

Here’s how it works: Cells pump charged particles (ions) in and out, creating voltage differences across their membranes. When one cell’s voltage spikes, neighbors detect the change and respond—like dominoes falling without touch. A wounded leaf sends distress signals this way, warning other leaves to flood themselves with bitter chemicals before insects arrive. Cut a flatworm in half, and bioelectric patterns guide its head and tail to regrow in the right places. No brain needed.

Scientists now hack these signals. Apply the right voltage to a frog embryo, and it grows an extra eye on its gut. Block the wrong channels in a tumor, and cancer cells forget how to spread. It’s not genetics or chemistry doing the talking—it’s physics.

Three related subjects are regenerative medicine secrets, plant internet networks, and how slime solves mazes.
